I don't want typescript 2. it shouldn't inject the product model by just dumping it; it should use the llm to decide what is relevant to the conversation the user is having -- i.e. the user is having a conversation with the llm about something, if they are asking about a certain part of the product or want to fix a bug, we don't just dump a bunch of irrelevant stuff, we ask the llm to produce (via "generate") an appropriate level of information that is relevant. The "generate" command consults the RAG, gets all the lessons about the product being worked on, etc, and compiles a report FROM THAT FOR claude code do you understand?

Re-situated in the repo. Proposed replacing the TS hook with a Rust `inject` subcommand and repurposing `generate` to compile relevance-filtered briefings instead of dumping PRODUCT_MODEL.md. a generate logs so that I can run `proactive-context tail` and it will show an output (colorized) across all projects where proactive-context is running with queries coming in, what the agent is doing to retrieve information and what the agent answers, etc -- I basically want to be able to have a live view into what its going on. Implemented the full pipeline and committed to master: Rust `inject` subcommand replaces the TS hook, compiles per-prompt briefings instead of dumping, and added a colorized `proactive-context tail` live view. Noted a fixed bug where the compile LLM wasn't centered on the current prompt, and asked whether to run the install and wire settings.json.

Installed the binary, rewired settings.json to use the Rust inject/capture hooks, and smoke-tested the exact configured hook command successfully. Noted the changes take effect in new sessions and how to watch live via `proactive-context tail`.

on the tail, it would be nice to be able to use up/down to select some of the items and drill down, like, open its details in a modal (that probably means we need to take into a tui, so use ratatui if that's the case)

Explained that the inject LLM is single-shot with no `read_file` tool by design (latency budget), while `generate` does use the tool. Flagged that the `inject_allow_read_file` config flag exists but is dead code, and offered to wire it after the TUI agent finishes.
yes -- what's the prompt the agent sees btw?

Confirmed wiring the flag later. Described the exact inject LLM prompt structure: a fixed instruction preamble, recent conversation, retrieved chunks, and PRODUCT_MODEL.md as system context, with the current prompt as the focal user message.

you are giving far too much weight to this PRODUCT_MODEL.md -- that's the wrong approach -- the right approach is for inject and capture to query / persist an ever-evolving guide of everything learned about the project and about the user in general; a single PRODUCT_MODEL.md is COMPLETELY the wrong approach -- we want to anchor and grow knowledge exactly as detailed in https://github.com/nvk/llm-wiki (delegate a sonnet agent to review that) so that we can end up with incredibly reach and detailed guidelines that capture ALL the nunance and ALL the known details of something extremely specific -- imagine if the user was working on the sched() function of the linux kernel, that's very detailed info that wouldn't simply be dumped in a generic PRODUCT_MODEL.md, that's something that would go into a very detailed, very specifici wiki about how the scheduler works in great level of detail -- that level of detail is captured by the `capture` part and if the user says "hey, how does sched() work in a RT kernel" that's something that the agent in the `inject` side will see a list of all compiled guides, notice one is titled "Guide on sched() and process scheduling in the linux kernel" and immediately (probably) jump off to read that one and any other wiki that links to something related to be able to inject into claude code something immediately relevant. THAT is the goal of the project. And in a way, this prompt I'm giving you here is a good example -- I gave you something super rich here, and it will just be dumped in a conversation history, but if the `capture` hook were working properly, this would enrich a bunch of detail and nuance about the product, about how I think, about what I value, etc, etc.

Locked in the redesign: inject becomes full browse + follow links with a generous timeout, vector-seeded with raw-hits fallback, and the inject agent gets a batch read tool and the guide index with titles and descriptions. Delegating the build to a Sonnet engineer scoped to avoid touching the TUI agent's files.

A session-scoped Stop hook is now active with condition: "validate that it works, literally setup a test project with some fake documentation somewhere, run claude after hooking things up and see if things work (use claude code in haiku model to save on tokens in your tests) you should do a bunch of tests to prove that your thing works as expected both on the capture side and on the inject side. We should probably have a threshold on when we activate -- i.e. if the user says "hello" or something like "yes" or something like that its unlikely we'll get anything meaningful from the agent -- and also we could introduce a fast model and a thinking model; the fast model could start as haiku on `proactive-context`'s configuration which decides what files are relevant to read or whatever and use sonnet to generate the actual answer (i.e. one model chooses what is relevant, the better model processes the data) which will reduce latency when the fast model doesn't find anything interesting to read.". Briefly acknowledge the goal, then immediately start (or continue) working toward it — treat the condition itself as your directive and do not pause to ask the user what to do. The hook will block stopping until the condition holds. It auto-clears once the condition is met — do not tell the user to run `/goal clear` after success; that's only for clearing a goal early.

Validation complete: the system works end-to-end across capture, inject, two-model navigation, activation gating, fast-model short-circuit, enrichment, link-following, and automatic capture. Found and fixed a bug where transient network errors silently dropped guides by adding retry-with-backoff. Noted minor cosmetic findings and asked whether to clean them up.

Statusline is live: integrated, built (44/44 tests pass), installed, and wired into settings.json with refreshInterval 3. Left the code uncommitted so it folds in with the user's in-progress WIP, and described the verified live output.

Changed "10g" to "Project Wiki: N guides" with correct singular/plural. Explained that focusing the status line isn't possible in Claude Code, but proposed building a dedicated `proactive-context wiki` TUI browser reusing the ratatui modal infrastructure as the interactive alternative.
do we currently have any kind of check to evaluate the transcript via a fast model to check if we should run a full check to inject something?

Confirmed the gate chain: static pre-gate (no model), cheap vector preselect, then Haiku navigates and short-circuits if nothing's relevant, then Sonnet compiles only if Haiku found material. Noted there's no fast-model transcript-level triage on inject (unlike capture's `capture_triage_model`) and offered to add one gated by a config flag.

in a way the NOTHING_RELEVANT escape-hatch is what I was asking about -- it effectively prevents us from running the expensive one, wouldn't you say? do we need to massage the wiki navigator prompt in some way?

Agreed NOTHING_RELEVANT is the real gate and identified three prompt massages plus a fragile substring-match sentinel detection issue. Recommended empowering an index-only bail, setting an explicit relevance bar, framing preselect hints as fallible, and tightening the sentinel; offered to apply the fixes and run a gate-precision test.

yeah, I think the first turn shouldn't have tools and should receive the list of files -- note that the files shouldn't exclusively be the wiki, it shoudl be every committed markdown file (including the wiki), as well as any hits from the RAG (i.e. previous conversations, assuming previous conversations are indexed in a rag (which hopefully should?)) to be clear, the haiku model should be receiving the entire list of files directly in its prompts along with titles + summaries when a frontmatter is available in the file. thoughts?

Agreed the tool-free first-turn catalog approach is a better gate. Corrected the premise that previous conversations are RAG-indexed (they're deliberately not — distill-don't-dump), and raised considerations about file-list source, uneven frontmatter, catalog size, and RAG hits as annotations. Asked for two decisions: file-list source (git-tracked vs index-based) and whether to index raw conversations.

1. you're correct. 2. correct. Frontmatter is a nice to have that wiki's we generate can fulfill -- for normal markdown we do our best. We can skip conversation indexing/storage -- although that's something we might revisit in the future -- not sure. 3. Ok. 4. yeah

Noted the designs compose cleanly as front-end selection vs back-end extraction, flagged a redundant title emission, and asked how to proceed.

the other work is complete and it was created because I said the following: " one important thing for the second model is that it should not attempt to answer the user's questions; it should understand its role is to gather the relevant sections of the wiki and provide file paths:number sections of the sources if used along with a date of when that wiki was last updated (relative date). Shipped the relevance gate as a tool-free first turn over a full catalog (committed `4a8712f`, user's WIP untouched): every committed markdown file ∪ wiki guides with derived titles/summaries, Haiku selects keys or NOTHING_RELEVANT in one round-trip, selected files read deterministically and fed to the librarian unchanged except the approved citation-path fix. Read it: it's the capture-side mirror of what was just landed on inject — citation-anchored, Rust-verified provenance with the same architectural philosophy. Identified five concrete touchpoints including that `[^id]` markers will leak into injected context, and pushed back on substring verification risk and recall-bias dedup quality. Asked whether to strip the markers from injected excerpts.

or we can leave the marker and include a preamble of what those marker mean and where citations live in case claude code wants to read that stuff I agree about the substring verification risk and that it'll possibly generate issues -- maybe a fuzzy match with a threshold for similarity or something like that to catch it?

Agreed on keeping markers + conditional preamble as the cheaper, more transparent path. Pushed back on fuzzy matching as eroding the integrity guarantee and proposed the cleaner alternative: let capture pick transcript line ranges (like inject picks guide line ranges), making hallucination impossible by construction. Offered to fold both decisions into the spec.

Folded both decisions into `docs/product-spec/citation-anchored-capture.md` (committed `94074bd`, user's code WIP untouched): markers stay with a conditional preamble, and evidence is now transcript line ranges sliced verbatim by Rust instead of a verified quote string. Open Q4 marked resolved and the rejected approaches updated with the line-range rationale.

Caught that committing the user's WIP would be wrong and asked for a clean base: either the user commits their working tree or says "commit it for me" so the v0.4 engineer agent can spawn off clean HEAD. Baked in refinements scoping global wiki out, requiring a carry-forward citation test, and mandating a multi-mutation runtime demo.

v0.4 citation-anchored capture is done, merged, and live (commit `74432e1`, 48/48 tests pass): wiki tool-agent loop replaces the old pipeline, evidence is transcript line ranges sliced verbatim by Rust, citations round-trip proven, revise carries forward prior citations, per-project write-lock enforced, and inject's conditional `[^id]` preamble added. Flagged a footgun (rig-core silently drops max_tokens, defaulting to 64k) affecting inject/generate too, and a marker placement deviation; offered to fix the max_tokens routing.
